author: @KathyWoodhouse-p7v
text: I would recommend magnesium sulphate paste as it has a drawing action. Very effective for removing splinters and thorns. Much less risky than your method.  Just apply a bit on a plaster and cover, may need to repeat in a couple of days for very stubborn thorns.

author: @ashtang2029
text:
Hello Sir,

We are a group of students participating in an Engineering project, our project is mainly generating energy from a Tribo-electric nanogenerator, we had seen  your video on TNT omnibus channel beforehand, we'd like your advice and help on making the triboelectric nanogenerator and how we'd connect multiple cells to maximise power production, we have many questions as well, like whether we'd be able to store this charge in a capacitor or a lithium ion battery and use it later on ? We have planned to make the TENG out of PTFE (plumber's tape) and Aluminium foil, moreover we weren't able to access the article mentioned in your video, any help would be instrumental and highly helpful
thank you

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text:
The best stuff for drawing out splinters and thorns from beneath the skin (and other skin issues) is Yellow Basilicon Ointment. Always have a jar. The genuine stuff is best but not Kosher as it contains lard. Earnshaw’s Herbal Dispensary is just one supplier who still adheres to the original recipe. 
Splinters of brass are another hazard in engineering workshops as the splinters cause the wound to fester. Its the copper based wood preservative on timber that does the same thing.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @mfree80286
text:
I've always had brilliant luck with a sewing needle dipped in iodine, which is probably wrong in some or many ways but hasn't failed yet. The key is light backpressure while holding the 'portal' open with a circling motion. 

Every 5-10 years though I manage to break a knife handle/blade holder and zip a finger open. Splash of IPA followed by oodles of the coldest clean water available has so far been enough to get the blood staunched and the wound sides to stick together well enough that bandage pressure and a splint will allow it to heal completely in a couple days. The cleaner the cut, the faster it mends...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @industrialmonk
text: I have been doing things like this since the mid 1980s as I didn't get sick pay. So rather than going to hospital to get a steel splinter cut out of my parm & having stitches. I did it with a rather cheeper scalpel with hot salted water to sterilise my scalpel & my hand before & after.

author: @GavinImpett
text: I had a toothpick splinter stuck in my heel and was reluctant to self-surgery, so I went to the ER.  I became an instant hero and was ushered to the front of the line. "A toothpick in your foot, how'd you manage that one? English, you say? Well that explains it."  And yes, there was a please don't, I really don't want to watch, but can't quite stop through the entire video. Well done. Nice patio, btw.

author: @Kargoneth
text:
Whaaat? No video of the construction process? Dagnabbit.

Glad you got that splinter out of your finger though. I usually either slit the skin with something sharp or weave a needle in and out along the surface layers of my skin and then pull the needle away to break the skin.

author: @donniewatson9120
text: You and I have the high pain tolerance in common. I've done the same thing with splinters and metal shards embedded in my fingers so many times in my nearly 56 years of life. My knife was a Tester modeling knife I got as a teen for trimming the edges of the plastic pieces after removing them from the frames they were attached to. Mine was one of the old ones that had the replaceable blades.

author: @AlanWatson-p3o
text: I'm a carpenter and intimately familiar with splinters of all shapes and sizes. Confirmed, if it can't be easily pulled or dug out, leave to ferment for a couple of days, remove scab and squeeze. Works every time 🌋

author: @grendel1960a
text: i use a standard modelling scalpel with swan morton blades to remove splinters, one trick is to put a dab of honey on a plaster and stick that over the wound, the honey will make the skin soft and stop infection, and often when you take the plaster off the splinter has either partially or wholly worked is way out. ps I find the heavy duty plasters are more likely to stay put through hand washing etc.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tips mate - thanks for sharing

author: @ashiesmum
text:
🌻ohhh.. I've just recently discovered the Metmo site. Wonderful. But you don't need that multi tool for that scalpel blade - that's a standard swann surgical blade, the right tool for that gruesome job 😹(worked with them in my labs).
I built my own decking but with  green sawn sweet chestnut from Shropshire, so no splinters 😸 yes always buying British/local😺 especially my timber.
Now what excuse can I find to buy something from Metmo......

The only thing I could recommend is using cyanoacrilate glue to close the wound, I did that when a broken dish sliced my finger close to the bone (I had done that before for other wounds, but that was the heaviest)

author: @totherarf
text:
Sometimes I worry ...... about myself!
I sit here at my laptop, dog comatose in  front of my log burner,  whilst I watch a guy talking about a bit of minor digit surgery. Why do I worry?
I worry because on the shelf of the table I sit at there is a similarly used item. Mine is a marking out knife, but equally sharp and good for removing errant bits from my body.




 Small world, eh?
